era_context: Sargon II's Neo-Assyrian Empire continues its dominance of the Near East. In China, the Spring and Autumn Period is characterized by increasing interstate conflict and the emergence of powerful regional hegemonies. Greece is developing the polis system and beginning to establish colonies throughout the Mediterranean.

events.title: Sargon II's campaigns in Anatolia and the Taurus Mountains
events.description: Sargon II directs military campaigns into Anatolia, particularly targeting the Taurus Mountains and the regions beyond. These campaigns aim to secure trade routes, suppress resistance from mountain peoples, and expand Assyrian territorial control. The campaigns are part of a broader strategy to create a defensible, economically integrated empire. Success in these campaigns strengthens Assyrian control over crucial trade networks connecting Mesopotamia to the Mediterranean and Anatolia.

sources.name: Sargon II's Annals and inscriptions from Dur-Sharrukin
Archaeological evidence from Assyrian sites and Anatolian excavations

events.certainty_note: Sargon II's Anatolian campaigns are attested in his inscriptions, but precise dating within his reign varies by ±1–2 years depending on chronological reconstruction.
cross_references: Part of broader Assyrian expansion -745 to -705

disconfirming_evidence: The extent and success of Sargon II's Anatolian campaigns are debated. Some scholars argue that resistance was stronger and more persistent than Assyrian inscriptions suggest. The reliability of Assyrian annalistic records for specific years is questioned; they may conflate or rearrange events for narrative effect.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 717 BCE is sparse and almost entirely dependent on Assyrian sources, which present a one-sided narrative. No contemporary records from conquered peoples survive. Chinese sources for this year are unreliable and often legendary. Most of the world has no written records for this period.
